title: Psalms 40
subtitle: He Put a New Song in My Mouth

sections.header: Verses 1–10 — “He Lifted Me Out of the Slimy Pit”

heb.paragraph: "He put a new song (shîr ḥādāsh) in my mouth, a hymn of praise to our God" (v.3). David waited patiently (v.1), God heard (v.1), lifted him from the pit (v.2), set his feet on rock (v.2), and gave him a new song (v.3). The rescue produces music. Then the stunning declaration: "Sacrifice and offering you did not desire — but my ears you have opened" (v.6). Hebrews 10:5–7 applies this to Christ’s incarnation.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

heb.paragraph: "But as for me, I am poor and needy; may the Lord think of me. You are my help and my deliverer; you are my God, do not delay (ḥūshâ)" (v.17). The psalm’s second half is a lament: troubles surround (v.12), sins overtake (v.12), David asks for swift rescue (v.13). Psalm 70 is nearly identical to vv.13–17 — preserved as an independent psalm.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: David is a key figure in He Put a New Song in My Mouth.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: God is a key figure in He Put a New Song in My Mouth.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVI proclaim your saving acts in the great assembly; I do not seal my lips, Lord, as you know.ESVI have told the glad news of deliverance in the great congregation; behold, I have not restrained my lips, as you know, O LORD.NIVSacrifice and offering you did not desire— but my ears you have opened— burnt offerings and sin offerings you did not require.ESVIn sacrifice and offering you have not delighted, but you have given me an open ear. Burnt offering and sin offering you have not required.

rows.label: Do Not Delay — 40:11–17
rows.text: "I am poor and needy." Sins overtake. "You are my help — do not delay."

hebtext.note: "He put a new song (shîr ḥādāsh) in my mouth, a hymn of praise to our God" (v.3). David waited patiently (v.1), God heard (v.1), lifted him from the pit (v.2), set his feet on rock (v.2), and gave him a new song (v.3). The rescue produces music. Then the stunning declaration: "Sacrifice and offering you did not desire — but my ears you have opened" (v.6). Hebrews 10:5–7 applies this to Christ’s incarnation.

"But as for me, I am poor and needy; may the Lord think of me. You are my help and my deliverer; you are my God, do not delay (ḥūshâ)" (v.17). The psalm’s second half is a lament: troubles surround (v.12), sins overtake (v.12), David asks for swift rescue (v.13). Psalm 70 is nearly identical to vv.13–17 — preserved as an independent psalm.

themes.note: Prophecy peaks: Hebrews 10:5–7 applies "sacrifice you did not desire" to Christ’s incarnation. Worship: the new song from the pit. Mercy: God lifts, sets, and gives music.
